Fern PolIrIcer- PRACTICEs CouulsSIoN
428 J Street r Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
August 22,2006
Elizabeth Wagner Hull
Assistant CityAttomey
City of Chula Vista
2T6FowthAvenue
Chula Vista, CA 91910
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-06-137
Dear Ms. Hull:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Chula Vista City
Councilmember Steve Castaneda regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the'Act").1 Because your request does not involve a particular
governmental decision, we will only provide informil assistance.2
QUESTION
Chula Vista City Councilmember Castaneda owns a primary residence in the
Urban Core Specific Plan ("UCSP") study area of Chula Vista (the "City''). Does the
Councilmernber's property interest create a conflict of interest that would preclude him
from voting with respect to decisions regarding the preparation of the City's UCSP?
CONCLUSION
It appears that Councilmember Castaneda has a conflict of interest regarding
decisions affecting the UCSP. However, it appears that the "public generally''exception
would permit Councilmember Castaneda to participate in the decision, despite his
conflict of interest, if either (l) l0% or more of all property owners or homeowners in the
city, or (2) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the City would be affected, and the
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18709-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831t4; regulation 18329(c)(3), copyenclosed).
iegeE


File No. I-06-137
Page No. 2
govemmental decisions in question would affecthis economic interest in substantially the
same manner as it would affedthe significant segment identified.
F'ACTS
The Community Development Department of the City is in the process of
preparing a UCSP. The UCSP will provide a neighborhood ievel plan io effectively
1Tnlel9nt the goals and policies foithe City's "rU* 
core as identified in the General
Plan. The UCSP will address, at the neighborhood level, land use mix and distribution,
zoning, urban and.alchi_tectural design, vehicular and peiestrian circulation,parting,
transit services and facilities, public improvements, and implementutio" pio"idures. The
UCSP affects the UCSP study area,theboundaries of which are: Interstaie 5 to the west,
Second Avenue to the east, C Street to the north; andL Street to the south. City
councilmember castaneda owns apimary residence in the ucsp study area-
Based on the current GIS database, there are approximately 75,g33 dwelling units
,n 9" city and approximately 15,2g7 units in the ucSp study ur"i 1-ioN). r,, ,, August
8, 2006 telephone conversation with city Attomey Ann Moore, she indicated that it was
unknown whether (or how many of) the dwelting units in the city or the UCSp study area
were owned by separate property owners or homeowners.
AIIALYSIS
The primary purpose of the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions is to ensure that
"[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by theirown financial interests or the financial
interests of persons,who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of
this goal, section s7100 of the Act prohibits a public official fr* -"r.i"g, participating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influenc" u gJr".nme.rtut
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section g7100 requires
analysis of the following questions as outlined below.
Steps One and Two: Is the individual a 6'public official,, and will he be
making, participating in making, or attemptingio influence a government decision?


File No. I-06-137
Page No. 3
Councilmember Castaneda is considered a "public official" and will be making,
participatrng in making, or influencing a governmental decision in voting on the
preparation of the UCSP.3 Note, if a council member has a conflict of interest in a
decision to be considered at a noticed public meeting, the council member must: (l)
immediatelyprior to the discussion of the item, verbally identifu each tlpe of economic
interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest as discussed in
regulation 18702.5(b)(1XB) on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself,
and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (See
regulation I 87 02.5, enclosed.)
Step Three: Identify the public officialrs economic interests.
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
mateial financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate farily," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investmenta of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management (section 37 I 03(d); reg. 187 03. I (b));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section s7103(b); reg. 18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87103(c); reg. 18703.3);
a
a
3 Apublic official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority
of his or her position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, oi
enters into any conhactual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87100; reg. 18702.1.) A
public official ':participates in making a govemmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or
her position and without significant substantive review, the official negotiates, advises or makes
recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87100; reg.
18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a decisionbefore his
or her own ageficy if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts or appears before any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Section 87100; reg. 181013.)
o An indirect investrnent or interest means any invesftnent or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by anagent on behalf of apublic official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined
at section 82029 as an offtcial's spouse and dependent children.


File No. I-06-137
Page No. 4
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
871 03(e); reg. 18703.4);
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including
those of his or her immediate family - this is the "personal financial effetts,, rule
(section 87103; reg. 15703.5).
We assume tlut th" official in question has an interest in his real property worth at
least $2,000. Therefore, the official has an economic interest in his real property. you
have not identified any other possible economic interest associated witfr tnis official.
Step Four: For each of the public official's economic interests, determine
whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision
which the public official wiII be making, participating in making, or using or
attempting to use his/her official position to influence.
Once an official identifies an economic interest, he or she must determine whether
it is "reasonably foreseeable" thatthe decision(s) in question will have a,.material
financial effect" on that interest. First, the official must decide whether the economic
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the specific decision in question. (Reg.
18700(b)(4).) Having established the degree of involvement, the official "u, ih".,
identiff the materiality standard appropriate to the circumstances. (Reg. 1S700(b)(5).)
The official then knows what financial effect would be considered "maierial', under the
Act. Finally, the official must decide whether such a material financial effect is a
"reasonably foreseeable" consequence of the decision(s) at issue. (Reg. 1g700(bx6).)
Real Property: Realproperty in which a public official has an economic interest
is directly involved in a governmental decision ifany of the following apply:
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real propertS is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposedboundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
govemmenta]_d.ecisfon. For pu{poses of subdivision (a)(5), real property
is located 'within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed bounaari"r; oi
the real property which is the subject of the gorr..n-er,tal decision, if any
part of the real property is within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment project area.


File No. I-06-137
Page No. 5
"(2)The governmental decision involves the zoningor rezoring,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property. For purposes of this
subdivision, the terms ozoring' and'rezoning' shall refer to the act of
establishing or changing the zoningor land use designation on the real
property in which the official has an interest.
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authoizinga
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an
interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property
in which the official has an interest.
fl...17
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
seryices." (Regulation 187 0a.2@).)
Councilmember Castaneda owns property in the study area. Thus, pursuant to
regulation 18704.2 (a)(2), his real property is directly involved in the decision(s) in
question.
Personal Financial Effecfs: With respect to personal financial effects on public
officials, regulation 18704.5 provides that:
"(a) A public official or his or her immediate family is deemed to
be directly involved in a governmental decision which has any financial
effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate
family."
Furthermore, regulation 1 8705. 5(a) provides that
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official,s
personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in arry r2-month period.
When determining whether a goveflrmental decision has a material
financial effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her
personal finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real property
owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the


File No. I-06-137
Page No. 6
gross revenues, expeflses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business
entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment interest shall
be considered."
Other than an analysis of personal financial effects, this analysis is focused on one
economic interest: Councilmember Castaneda's personal residence. Regulation 18705.5
states that whether a goveflrmental decision has a material financial effect on a public
official's personal finances, financial effects on his real property value shall not be
considered. Thus, any personal financial effect on Councilmember Castaneda,s residence
shall be disregarded through the rest of this analysis.
Steps Five and Six: Materiality and Foreseeability
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is ..material.,,
(Reg. 18700(a).) An effect on an economic interest is considered "reasonably
foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Reg. l870O1uy.; a
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must
be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 Fppc ops. 19g.)
Real Property: Whenreal property is directly involved in a govemmental
decision, as here, there is a presumption under the Commission's regulations that the
financial effect of the decision upon the real property is material. (Reg. t8705.z(a)(l)
and (a)Q).) This presumption can only be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonabiy 
-
foreseeable that the decisions will have any financial effect at all on the real property.
(Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).) Since no proof to the contrary has been provided, for
purposes of this letter, we will assume that any financial interest will be material.
A financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is
substantially likely that it will occur as a result of the govemmental decision. (Reg.
18706(a).)
Step Seven: WilI the Governmental Decision Have a Financial Effect on the
Public Official's Economic Interests that is Indistinguishable from the Effect on the
Public Generally?
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in the decision under the "public generally''exception. This exception applies
if a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction or the official's election districtis affectea
by the govemmental decision in "substantially the same manner" as it would affect the
public official, the public official mayparticipate in the decision. (Section 87103; Reg.
t8707(a).)


File No. I-06-137
Page No. 7
Real Property, Significant SegmenL' Councilmember Castaneda has a conflict of
interest based on his interest in his real property. Specific subdivisions of
regulation 18707.1define what constitutes a "significant segment,,, based on the
economic interest involved. When the economic interest *hi"h gives rise to the conflict
of interest is an interest in real propertS the significant segment is defined as:
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners
in the jurisdiction of the official's uge*ybr the district the official
represents; or
"(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of
the officia|s agency." (Reg. r87o7.r(b)(lxBxi) - (ii).)
You note that Councilmember Castaneda's personal residence is within the UCSp
study area. You n9!e_tha! there are approximat ely is,s3z dwelling units in the city and
approximately 15,287 units in the UCSP study area which equates to approximat eiy 20
percent of the dwelling units in the City. Assuming that l0 percent or rriop of the
homeowners or property owners in the city are in i-he ucsp study area (or 5,000
property owners or-homeowners), they would constitute a significant segment. However,
you do not have information regarding whether (or how many ofl the dwelling units in
the city or the ucsP study area are owned by separate property owners or homeowners.
Substantially the Same Monner: If youcan determine that a significant segment
will be affected by a decision, it is next necessary to evaluate if the pub[c official,s
economic interest will be affected in substantially the same manner as the rest of the
segment. If the answer is "yes," then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally and the public official may particrpate in tie decision.
(Reg' 18707b)g)) To assess whether a significant segment will bi financially affected
in substantially the same manner, all measurable effects from the decision must be
identified. comparing financiar effects is necessarily a factual process.
As noted above, Councilmember Castaneda has an interest in real property
located within the area. Ifhe determines that a significant segment ofproperty owners or
homeowners will be financially affected by the diisions in a manner substaniially
:'-il* to the financial effect on his real propertS the "public generally', exception would
apply.
Step Eight: fs Councilmember Castaneda Legally Required to participate in
the Decision?
Section 87101 permits officials otherwise disqualified from making governmental
decisions to participate in decisions when their participation is legally r"qriirla. This is
the case if a quorum can not be achievedwithout the participation of disqualified public
officials' The rule does not apply when there is an alternative source of decisionmaking


File No. I-06-137
Page No. 8
consistent with the-statute authoizing.the decision. (Reg. 1g70g, copy enclosed.) Also,
itdoes not apply wfien the disqualified official's vote is Inlyneeded to break a tie or
when quorum can be convened among members not disqualified.
The "legally required participation" rule allows the participation of the smallest
number of disqualified person. t"""s.ary to constitute a quorum . (rn re Hudson (lg7g) 4
FPPC ops' 13.) The best random method of selecting which disquilifiea 111"1nu", should
participate is by lot. other means of random selectioi that areimpartial and equitable
may also be used. whatever method is used, all disqualified officiar. -".t purticipate in
the random selection and all must be equallylikely to be chosen. (ueisiige'raari""
Letter, No. 4-95-333.)
once it is determined which disqualified official will participate in a decision, that
official is selected for the duration of the proceedings in all related matters unless an
official who was previously disqualified no longer fr'ur u nrurcial interest in the decision.
A disqualified official who participates under the authority of section g7101 may
participate fully in the matter, including taking part in deliberations, voting in open
sessions 9f the body and in such closed sessions as is required by law. However, the
reinstated official may not attempt to influence the outco:me of the matter ,.behind the
g.:_y'.lby engaging in private discussions with other members o,,tutc 1n"f-
18708(bX4); Grunwald Advice Letter, No. A_95_1g4.)
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
y: 
Andreas C.
Counsel, Legal Division

substantially likely that it will occur as a result of the govemmental decision. (Reg.
18706(a).)
Step Seven: WilI the Governmental Decision Have a Financial Effect on the
Public Official's Economic Interests that is Indistinguishable from the Effect on the
Public Generally?
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in the decision under the "public generally''exception. This exception applies
if a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction or the official's election districtis affectea
by the govemmental decision in "substantially the same manner" as it would affect the
public official, the public official mayparticipate in the decision. (Section 87103; Reg.
t8707(a).)
Real Property, Significant SegmenL' Councilmember Castaneda has a conflict of
interest based on his interest in his real property. Specific subdivisions of
regulation 18707.1define what constitutes a "significant segment,,, based on the
economic interest involved. When the economic interest *hi"h gives rise to the conflict
of interest is an interest in real propertS the significant segment is defined as:
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners
in the jurisdiction of the official's uge*ybr the district the official
represents; or
"(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of
the officia|s agency." (Reg. r87o7.r(b)(lxBxi) - (ii).)
You note that Councilmember Castaneda's personal residence is within the UCSp
study area. You n9!e_tha! there are approximat ely is,s3z dwelling units in the city and
approximately 15,287 units in the UCSP study area which equates to approximat eiy 20
percent of the dwelling units in the City. Assuming that l0 percent or rriop of the
homeowners or property owners in the city are in i-he ucsp study area (or 5,000
property owners or-homeowners), they would constitute a significant segment. However,
you do not have information regarding whether (or how many ofl the dwelling units in
the city or the ucsP study area are owned by separate property owners or homeowners.
Substantially the Same Monner: If youcan determine that a significant segment
will be affected by a decision, it is next necessary to evaluate if the pub[c official,s
economic interest will be affected in substantially the same manner as the rest of the
segment. If the answer is "yes," then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally and the public official may particrpate in tie decision.
(Reg' 18707b)g)) To assess whether a significant segment will bi financially affected
in substantially the same manner, all measurable effects from the decision must be
identified. comparing financiar effects is necessarily a factual process.
As noted above, Councilmember Castaneda has an interest in real property
located within the area. Ifhe determines that a significant segment ofproperty owners or
homeowners will be financially affected by the diisions in a manner substaniially
:'-il* to the financial effect on his real propertS the "public generally', exception would
apply.
Step Eight: fs Councilmember Castaneda Legally Required to participate in
the Decision?
Section 87101 permits officials otherwise disqualified from making governmental
decisions to participate in decisions when their participation is legally r"qriirla. This is
the case if a quorum can not be achievedwithout the participation of disqualified public
officials' The rule does not apply when there is an alternative source of decisionmaking
consistent with the-statute authoizing.the decision. (Reg. 1g70g, copy enclosed.) Also,
itdoes not apply wfien the disqualified official's vote is Inlyneeded to break a tie or
when quorum can be convened among members not disqualified.
The "legally required participation" rule allows the participation of the smallest
number of disqualified person. t"""s.ary to constitute a quorum . (rn re Hudson (lg7g) 4
FPPC ops' 13.) The best random method of selecting which disquilifiea 111"1nu", should
participate is by lot. other means of random selectioi that areimpartial and equitable
may also be used. whatever method is used, all disqualified officiar. -".t purticipate in
the random selection and all must be equallylikely to be chosen. (ueisiige'raari""
Letter, No. 4-95-333.)
once it is determined which disqualified official will participate in a decision, that
official is selected for the duration of the proceedings in all related matters unless an
official who was previously disqualified no longer fr'ur u nrurcial interest in the decision.
A disqualified official who participates under the authority of section g7101 may
participate fully in the matter, including taking part in deliberations, voting in open
sessions 9f the body and in such closed sessions as is required by law. However, the
reinstated official may not attempt to influence the outco:me of the matter ,.behind the
g.:_y'.lby engaging in private discussions with other members o,,tutc 1n"f-

QUESTION: Chula Vista City Councilmember Castaneda owns a primary residence in the
Urban Core Specific Plan ("UCSP") study area of Chula Vista (the "City''). Does the
Councilmernber's property interest create a conflict of interest that would preclude him
from voting with respect to decisions regarding the preparation of the City's UCSP?

CONCLUSION: It appears that Councilmember Castaneda has a conflict of interest regarding
decisions affecting the UCSP. However, it appears that the "public generally''exception
would permit Councilmember Castaneda to participate in the decision, despite his
conflict of interest, if either (l) l0% or more of all property owners or homeowners in the
city, or (2) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the City would be affected, and the
